coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Multi-mode Quantum Dots Encoded Molecularly Imprinted Polymers Microspheres and Application in Quantitative Detection for Dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaolei
Cui, Yan
He, Yu
Wang, Shuo
Wang, Junping
coredata.dc:description: As the promising analytical strategy, multi-mode quantum dots (QDs) encoded molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors have attracted considerable attention. Here, a novel encoding strategy for water-dispersed QDs molecularly imprinted polymers microspheres (MIPMs) with a high-performance fluorescence intensity and multi-color signal was established. The water-compatible MIPMs were firstly synthesized via integrating the reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer and precipitation polymerization (RAFT-PP), which could improve the water compatibility by grafting the hydrophilic groups of poly(2-hydroxyrthyl methacrylate) (PHEMA). Then the water-compatible MIPMs were used as embedding matrix to separately incorporate with five different QDs, and a series of multi-color QDs encoded MIPs (five single-color and 10 dual-color fluorescent systems) were obtained. The fluorescent characterization of encoded MIPMs was studied in detail. Further, dual-mode fluorescence systems were fabricated as fluorescence sensors for the quantitative detection of dopamine; good linear response in the range of 5-300 μg L-1 and 1-100 μg L-1 and the limit of detection (LOD) with 2 μg L-1 and 0.5 μg L-1 were obtained for monochromatic and ratiometric sensor, respectively. It is the first time to introduce the optical encoding strategy into the fabrication of fluorescent MIPs, which not only greatly simplifies the preparation process of fluorescent imprinted sensor, but also provides more alternatives for the detection method of analyte and the potential of high-throughput multiple analytes at one time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Encoded microspheres
Quantum dot
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Fluorescent sensor
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of fluorescent sensor and detection procedure of DA
Characterization of single-color QDs encoded MIPs
Monochromatic CD-MIPs sensor for the DA detection
Effect of the addition amount of CDs on fluorescence intensity and adsorption capacity for DA
Analytical performance of the CD-MIPs sensor
Dual-emission ratiometric sensor for quantification detection of DA
The ratio optimization of N-GQD-BIPs and 605-MIPs
Analysis of DA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: True-color fluorescent images under a 365 nm UV lamp of (A) pure QDs and (B) encoded MIPs. (C) Fluorescent micrographs of encoded MIPs. Fluorescent spectra of (D) pure QDs and (E) encoded MIPs. From left to right, the pure QDs are N-GQDs, CD, 525-QDs, 585-QDs and 605-QDs in PBS, ethanol, PBS, PBS and hexane solution, respectively, and corresponding to encoded MIPs in PBS buffer.
FE-TEM mapping images of (A) original MIPs, (B) N-GQD-MIPs, (C) CD-MIPs, (D) 525-MIPs, (E) 585-MIPs, and (F) 605-MIPs.
The fluorescent spectra of the dual-emission fluorescent system in PBS buffer by mixing different two encoded MIPs.

(A) Fluorescent micrographs of encoded MIPs with different amounts of CDs (a-1 to a-6: 0 μ L, 20 μ L, 100 μ L, 200 μ L, 500 μ L). (B) Effect of different amounts of CDs on fluorescent intensity in PBS; FLMIPs/FL NIPs denotes the ratio of fluorescence intensity of CD-MIPs to CD-NIPs. (C) Binding capacity of CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs (10 mg) encoded with different amounts of CDs for 50 mg L-1 DA solution in PBS; IF denotes the ratio of the binding capacity of CD-MIPs to that of CD-NIPs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-MIPs (A) and CD-NIPs (C) in different concentrations DA, correspond to the calibration curve of CD-MIPs (B) and CD-NIPs (D).

The fluorescence emission spectra of ratiometric fluorescent MIPs (A) and NIPs (C) sensor by the addition of DA. The concentration of DA was 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 300 μg L-1 from up to down. Calibration curve of fluorescence response of △(I445/I605) versus the concentrations of DA for MIPs (B) and NIPs (D) sensors.
The fluorescence response of (A and B) CD-MIPs sensors and (C and D) ratiometric fluorescence sensors on DA and other interfering substances.
(A) Preparation procedure of MIPs; (B) the process of QDs encoded MIPs.
Comparison of proposed sensor with other reported methods of DA detection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Multi-mode Quantum Dots Encoded Molecularly Imprinted Polymers Microspheres and Application in Quantitative Detection for Dopamine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
College of Food Science and Engineering
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 29 The Thirteenth Road, Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the promising analytical strategy, multi-mode quantum dots (QDs) encoded molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors have attracted considerable attention. Here, a novel encoding strategy for water-dispersed QDs molecularly imprinted polymers microspheres (MIPMs) with a high-performance fluorescence intensity and multi-color signal was established. The water-compatible MIPMs were firstly synthesized via integrating the reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer and precipitation polymerization (RAFT-PP), which could improve the water compatibility by grafting the hydrophilic groups of poly(2-hydroxyrthyl methacrylate) (PHEMA). Then the water-compatible MIPMs were used as embedding matrix to separately incorporate with five different QDs, and a series of multi-color QDs encoded MIPs (five single-color and 10 dual-color fluorescent systems) were obtained. The fluorescent characterization of encoded MIPMs was studied in detail. Further, dual-mode fluorescence systems were fabricated as fluorescence sensors for the quantitative detection of dopamine; good linear response in the range of 5-300 μg L-1 and 1-100 μg L-1 and the limit of detection (LOD) with 2 μg L-1 and 0.5 μg L-1 were obtained for monochromatic and ratiometric sensor, respectively. It is the first time to introduce the optical encoding strategy into the fabrication of fluorescent MIPs, which not only greatly simplifies the preparation process of fluorescent imprinted sensor, but also provides more alternatives for the detection method of analyte and the potential of high-throughput multiple analytes at one time.

ce:section.ce:para: Encoding microspheres are of great significant in high-throughput multiplexed bioassays, which could simultaneously identify multiple targets in a single system [1,2]. Because of the simplicity, rapid readout and low cost, optical encoding microspheres have been easily achieved by embedding semiconductor quantum dots (denoted semi-QDs) into microspheres, providing the uniqueness emission spectra [3,4]. Since the concept of semi-QDs encoding was first presented in 2001 by the Nie group [5], semi-QDs encoded beads have been highly widespread concerned, and have shown remarkable capabilities and multi-color signals in optical encoding. For instance, Song et al. put forward the gradual solvent evaporation method to synthesize the high-performance QDs-encoded polymer beads in 2011 [6]; Wang et al. developed a swelling-evaporation approach for the efficient incorporation of QDs into porous microspheres in 2012 [7]; Song et al. further presented a new self-healing encapsulation strategy to generate the functionalized uniform quantum dots barcodes to improve the chemical stability and solve the QDs leakage problem in 2014 [8]; and Kim et al. proposed a concentration-controlled flow-focusing technique to prepare QDs barcode for diagnosis of patients in 2016 [9]. Silica, polystyrene, and other highly cross-linked monodisperse porous microspheres are the most commonly used embedding matrix, yet, they are absence of selectivity for targets of interest. Because of the complex nature of the sample matrices and trace level targets, the specifically recognition for targets is a necessary requirement.

Molecular imprinting has been developed to be a well-established technology to produce synthetic receptors with particular template-shaped cavities [10]. Molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor constructed by combining molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with fluorescent materials possesses significant features of high sensitivity, quick response and easy operation, being regarded as the promising analytical strategy in the field of biological and chemical analyte detection [11,12]. Although significant progress has been made, the proposed fluorescent imprinted microspheres are mainly single-color fluorescent signal [10,13,14], which is difficult to be used for multi-object detection and accurate quantitative detection. Another, MIPs for organic small molecule are all synthesized in non-aqueous phase, and oil-soluble quantum dots is commonly directly used as fluorescent materials since the water-soluble QDs with the poor stability in organic solvent are not compatible with the preparing system of traditional MIPs [15,16]. However, the fluorescence MIPs sensors embedded oil-soluble QDs always exhibit low fluorescence intensity and bad transparency [17]. Additionally, the currently fluorescence MIPs are mostly compatible with organic solvent, which could lead to the aggregation of polymers and disappearance of the specific binding in water medium or biological buffer. Therefore, the application of molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensors is limited, especially for the detection of target objects in biological samples and quantitative detection.

To meet the demand of biological analysis, functionalization of imprinted polymers is a frequently used approach, such as surface carboxylic or hydroxyl modification [8,18]. Very recently, we have reported the synthesis of water-compatible imprinted polymers by using the reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) technique with reflux precipitation polymerization (RPP) to solve the problem of water compatible, which provided the hydrophilic brushes (poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate)) on the surface of MIPs [19]. Owing to the employment of RAFT technique, the hydrophilic brushes (poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate)) was grafted on the surface of MIPs. The obtained water-compatible MIPs not only exhibited well water dispersibility, but uniform sphere structure, high specific surface area, and excellent specific recognition for template in the pure water medium. These features suggest that the water-compatible MIPs have great potential to be a novel embedding matrix for preparing multi-color encoded imprinted sensors to address the aforementioned issues.

Besides, as new quantum dots are synthesized, more and more fluorescent materials can be used in fluorescent sensors, such as semi-QDs [20,21], carbon dots (CDs) [22,23], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [24], and so on. Compared with semi-QDs, CDs and GQDs are the newest members of the quantum dots, and emerged as the superior fluorophores because of the key merits, including the low toxicity, small size, biocompatibility, high tunable photoluminescence (PL) property and chemical inertness [25,26]. The appearances of these new quantum dots will facilitate the development of multiple-color encoding technology and the fabrication of fluorescent sensors with multiple signals. However, the currently development of encoding strategy proposed by Nie group all focused on the application of oil-soluble semi-QDs, since the incompatible of hydrophobicity embedding matrix and water-soluble QDs is still a difficult task, yet, limiting the application of other various polar fluorescent materials. Surprisingly, employing the water-compatible MIPs as embedding backbone makes it easily ensure the embedding matrix and different polarity QDs are both well dispersed simultaneously.

Dopamine (DA) is an important neurotransmitter in the central nervous and cardiovascular systems [27]. Several important diseases, such as Parkinson’s disease, are related to dysfunctions in dopamine regulation. Up to now, different analytical methods have been developed for the DA detection, including the classical high performance liquid chromatography [28], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [29], electrochemical [30], spectrophotometry [31], and capillary electrophoresis techniques [32]. Because of the easily oxidative nature of DA, electrochemical approach is commonly used for the DA detection. However, other interferences (such as ascorbic acid and uric acid) has a 100–1000 times higher than that of the DA level, and they undergo oxidation with a same potential peak as DA [33,34]. Because of the complex matrices and low level in biological samples, the development of high sensitivity and selectivity sensors for the quantification of DA is essential.

Inspired these, functionalized water-compatible MIPs were first employed as a novel embedding matrix for preparing well water-dispersed QDs encoded MIPs with different color and fluorescence spectra, and two mode of those were further used as fluorescent sensors for dopamine. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , first, the water-compatible MIPs backbone with uniform spherical structure and large specific surface area were prepared using an approach of combining reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization and precipitation polymerization (denoted RAFT-PP) (see Scheme 1A). Second, the above MIPs backbone as embedding matrix were dispersed in a solution of different QDs and stirred for incorporating QDs into MIPs backbone (see Scheme 1B). After removal solvent, five QDs encoded imprinted polymers with multicolor fluorescence signals were separately achieved. Finally, dual-mode fluorescent sensors, including monochromatic and dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor, were successfully fabricated for the quantitative detection of DA. The objective of this study put forward a novel strategy for the fabrication of fluorescent sensor to solve the complex and incompatibility of reaction system in preparation process, and meanwhile provided the multi-mode fluorescent sensing system for analyte detection, which lays foundation for the establishment of high-throughput multi-objective detection platform.

Dopamine (DA), epinephrine (EP), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), caffeic acid (CA), and phenethylamine (PEA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA); the chemical structures of standards substances were shown in Fig. S1. Methacrylic acid (MAA), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA), and divinylbenzene (DVB) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Chain transfer reagent of 2-methyl-2-[(dodecylsulfanylthiocarbonyl)sulfanyl]propanoic acid (TTC) was supplied by Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CdSe/ZnS-605 semi-QDs with size of 5–10 nm in hexane, carboxyl group functionalized ZnCdSe/ZnS-525 semi-QDs and ZnCdSe/ZnS-585 semi-QDs with hydrodynamic diameter of 12–18 nm in borate buffer solution were provided by Wuhan Jiayuan Quantum Dots Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China), these synthesized processes followed the literatures [35,36]. The concentrations of these three semi-QDs were all 8 μmol L-1. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with diameter distribution of 0.5−4 nm in aqueous solution (N-GQDs, the concentration was 30 mg mL-1) and carbon quantum dots with average diameter of 1.5 nm in ethanol (CDs, the concentration was 100 mg mL-1) were synthesized according to the literature methods (see supplementary information) [33,37]. Poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) (PHEMA) polymer with a dithioester end group was also detailed in the supplementary information. MAA, HEMA, and AIBN were purified following the literature method [38].

Field emission high resolution transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin TEM at 200kv, FEI, Netherlands), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU1510, Hitachi, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor27, Bruker, Germany), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TGA-Q50, Waters, USA), autosorb IOA3200-4 nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Quantatech Co., USA), ultraviolet-visible spectrometry (Evolution 300, Thermo Scientific, USA) and fluorescence spectrometer (Lumina, Thermo Scientific, USA) were used to characterize the polymers.

The water-compatible MIP microspheres were synthesized by a combination of reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer and precipitation polymerization (RAFT-PP). Typically, 71 mg of DA as template, 127.8 μL of MAA as functional monomer, 1.125 mL of DVB as cross-linking agent, 150 mg of PHEMA as hydrophilic macromolecular chain-transfer agents (macro-CTAs), 20 mg of TTC as normal RAFT agent, and 10 mg of AIBN as initiator were dissolved in a mixed solution of methanol/acetonitrile (30 ml, 1:5, v/v) in a 50 mL round-bottomed flask. After ultrasonication for 10 min and purging with nitrogen for 20 min, the reaction system was sealed under nitrogen atmosphere and induced at 60 °C for 24 h under continuous magnetic stirring. After centrifugation, the obtained polymers were collected and transferred into a Soxhlet device (methanol: acetic acid (9:1, v/v)) to completely remove template. The resulting polymers were washed with methanol and water until the pH changed to neutral, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum. As a control, non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) were prepared using the same procedure in the absence of DA template.

Five fluorescence QDs were selected as embedding elements, including three water-soluble QDs (N-GQDs, ZnCdSe/ZnS-525, and ZnCdSe/ZnS-585) and two oil-soluble QDs (CDs and CdSe/ZnS-605). The general encoded QDs into MIPs by the directly impregnating method as follows:

Typically, 50 mg of MIP or NIP particles, 5 mL of ethanol (for oil-soluble QDs) or phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer (for water-soluble QDs), and a controlled amount of QDs were separately put in a 20 mL brown glass bottle and dispersed completely by ultrasonic waves for 5 min to form a homogeneous suspension, and then stirred magnetically for 12 h. After removing solvent using a sand core funnel (G5 type), five QDs encoded MIPs were separately obtained, by denoted N-GQD-MIPs, CD-MIPs, 525-MIPs, 585-MIPs and 605-MIPs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescent sensor and detection procedure of DA
ce:section.ce:para: For different purposes, the fluorescent sensors were constructed by two strategies. To establish monochromatic fluorescent sensor, 10 mg of CD-MIPs were dispersed into 10 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 8) as stock solution. Then 200 μL of the stock solution was added to 800 μL PBS buffer as working solution. To establish dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor, 5 mg of the N-GQD-MIPs were firstly suspended into 50 mg L-1 DA solution in PBS, and stirred for 2 h. The resulting polymers were denoted by N-GQD-BIPs. Then the 0.03 mg mL-1 of N-GQD-BIPs and 0.18 mg mL-1 of 605-MIPs in 1 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 8) were directly mixed for spectral measurement.

The detection procedure of DA as follows: 1 mL of the as-prepared working solution was added into 5 mL centrifuge tube, by adding different amount of DA. The final concentrations of DA were 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 300 μg L-1 for single-color CD-MIPs sensor, and 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 μg L-1 for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The mixed system was shaken for 100 min at room temperature on an oscillator and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C, then which was removed from the water bath to record the fluorescence spectra.

The fluorescence spectra of CD-MIPs and dual-emission fluorescence sensor were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and 330 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was an average of three independent measurements.

As the control experiments, the fluorescence response (F 0/F for CD-MIPs sensor and △(I445/I605) for ratiometric sensor) were evaluated with various interfering substance in PBS buffer at the same conditions, including some common ions (Na+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cl-, NO3 - and SO4 2-), amino acids (Val, Phe, Gly, Cys, Pro, Lys and His), various sugars (fructose, glucose and sucrose), structural analogues (EP, CA and PEA) and other co-existed substances (AA, UA, and urea), respectively. The mass concentration of interfering substance and DA was 300 μg L-1, except for the 66.7 mg L-1 for Na+, 76 mg L-1 for Cl-, 1 mg L-1 for Ca2+, 4 mg L-1 for K+, and 186 mg L-1 for urea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of single-color QDs encoded MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization and precipitation polymerization (PP) were employed for preparing the water-compatible MIPs to improve the water compatibility of imprinted polymers (see Fig. S3, S4, reaction process of RAFT in supplementary information). It has been proved that MIPs have large specific surface area (352.97 m2 g-1); uniform spherical structure and high thermal stability for the preparation of QDs encoded MIPs (see Fig. S5, characterization of polymers in supplementary information).

To validate the encoding strategy and the compatibility of different polar QDs, the fluorescence performance and fluorescence intensity were first studied, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, pure QDs and corresponding to QDs encoded MIPs all exhibited remarkable bright fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV lamp, suggesting the successful encoding process and “universal” encoding ability for QDs of differing polarity. A similar result was obtained from the fluorescence microscope images, shown in Fig. 1C. During the encoding process, the porous structure of embedding matrix plays an important role for the formation of high–performance fluorescence bead, which could promote QDs to penetrate deeply into the embedding matrix and improve the bright of resulting encoded beads [39]. Here, the large specific surface area and high adsorption performance of MIPs ensure that QDs could be easily absorbed to enter the matrix support and captured into the pore walls of MIPs by non-covalent interaction, such as electrostatic and hydrophobic interaction [40,41]. Fig. 1B also indicated the well water-dispersity of the obtained QDs encoded MIPs. Fig. 1E showed the corresponding photoluminescence spectra of single encoded MIPs. Compared with the pure QDs (see Fig. 1D), the peak profiles do not change in the encoding process.

To investigate the distribution of QDs in the encoded MIPs, the field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) mapping of C, N, O, Si, S, and Zn elements on original MIPs and encoded MIPs were recorded; the results were displayed in Fig. 2 . It is clearly that the characterization element of five QDs homogeneously distributed over the whole encoded microspheres, instead of locating on the surface of microspheres. It is evidence the QDs have been absorbed into the entire MIPs. There are three advantages for the QDs distributing over the whole encoded microspheres should be emphasized. First, the amount of encoded QDs distributing over the whole microspheres is greatly increased than that of the QDs only locating on the surface, leading to the higher fluorescent intensity of resulting encoded microspheres. Second, the chemical stability of encoded microspheres to prevent the QDs from leaking is greatly improved because the QDs were bound over the whole microspheres. In fact, the problem for the encoded microspheres with QDs located on the surface of microspheres is that QDs are easily “washed out” during the application process [42]. Third, the uniform distribution of QDs over the whole microspheres could ensure the repeatability of encoding process. In the five batches of synthesized microspheres, the fluorescence intensity is no significant difference (see Fig. S6), and the coefficient of variation (CV, %) was 3.52% and 2.86% for encoding CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs, respectively.

In addition, original MIPs and QDs encoded MIPs all exhibited uniform spherical structure, indicating the encoding process is relatively mild and does not affect the spherical structure of the original MIPs; this result was also confirmed in the SEM images of encoded MIPs shown in Fig. S7. Besides, the average particle sizes of MIPs, N-GQD-MIPs, CD-MIPs, 525-MIPs, 585-MIPs, and 605-MIPs were 2.66 μm, 2.89 μm, 2.72 μm, 2.81 μm, 2.71 μm, and 3.14 μm, respectively. It is obvious that the diameter of encoded MIPs was higher than that of the original MIPs, revealing that the QDs were captured into the inner of MIPs under the encoding process.

The successful fabrication of five different quantum dots encoding imprinted polymers indicated the successful combination of optical encoding technology and molecular imprinting technology. Compared with other traditional method, our proposed strategy greatly simplifies the preparation process, which not only does not require the complex modification of functional group for QDs, but the properties of QDs are not affected by the reaction system of MIPs since the synthesized process of MIPs and QDs encoding process are separated.

In principle, more usable code could generate by using more colors or more intensity levels. Following the above single-color barcode studied, several dual-color fluorescence systems were further studied by directly mixing two encoded MIPs with different color. The using of five QDs at a constant intensity level could generate ten dual-color recognizable code. As shown in Fig. 3 , eight dual-color systems with well resolved individual emission peaks were obtained, and overlap between the two emission peaks was occurred for the combination of N-GQD-MIPs and CD-MIPs, as the same phenomenon with the combination of 585-MIPs and 605-MIPs. Generally, a 30 nm interspace between the two emission wavelengths is required for spectrophotometer to effectively distinguish the two QDs [43]. In the visual region (380 nm–780 nm), 10–12 different colors were allowed to be resolved with acceptable spectral overlap [44,45]. It is evidence that more-color fluorescent systems were also conducted by mixing more encoded MIPs with different color, not just the dual-color system.

In summary, five single-color and ten dual-color fluorescent sensing systems were simultaneously obtained, which illustrated the “universal” of encoding process on one hand, and provided more alternatives for the detection method of analyte on the other hand. The single-color sensing systems are more suitable for rapid DA detection, and the dual-color fluorescent systems are commonly used for the high sensitivity and visual recognition by the naked eyes [46]. More importantly, the multi-color fluorescent sensing indicated the potential of multiple analytes detection at one time by mixing various color encoded imprinted microspheres of different analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monochromatic CD-MIPs sensor for the DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent response of N-GQD-MIPs, CD-MIPs, 525-MIPs, 585-MIPs, and 605-MIPs by the addition of 0-100 μg L-1 DA solution was first evaluated, respectively (see Fig. S8). Aside from the insensitive of N-GQD-MIPs, the fluorescence intensity of CD-MIPs, 525-MIPs, 585-MIPs and 605-MIPs were all quenched by the addition of DA (the quenching and detection mechanism was shown in supplementary information). Considering the wider quenching efficiency and excellent optical properties of CDs, monochromatic CD-MIPs system was selected as a target fluorescent sensor to explore the adsorption performance, sensitivity, selectivity and feasibility of DA detection in the subsequent experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the addition amount of CDs on fluorescence intensity and adsorption capacity for DA
ce:section.ce:para: During the fluorescence encoding process, the addition amount of doping CDs is an important factor. At the low amount of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of encoded polymers is limited and cannot achieve the detection requirement. Conversely, too many CDs could induce decreases in the sensor sensitivity and the binding capacity of polymers. Fig. 4 A showed fluorescence microscope images of CD-MIPs under encoding with different addition amounts of CDs; it is obvious that the fluorescence color becomes much brighter with increasing amounts of carbon dots until a quantity of 200 μL, and no obvious difference was found at a much higher concentration. This result was also confirmed from Fig. 4B, which showed linearly increasing fluorescence intensity for quantities of CDs ranging from 0 to 200 μL. This indicates that it is easy to predict and fabricate fluorescent CD-MIPs with precise fluorescence control by adjusting the quantities of CDs added. Meanwhile, it was also found the fluorescence intensity ratio (FLMIPs/FLNIPs) increased with amounts of CDs ranging 0 to 200 μL, and CD-MIPs produced much greater fluorescence intensity than did CD-NIPs when the quantity of CDs was greater than 200 μL. The reasons may be that some CDs were captured into the imprinted cavities of MIPs, which could be explained through the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of CD-MIPs (see Fig. S10). Comparing with the original MIPs (352.97 m2 g-1), a significantly decrease on the specific surface area of CD-MIPs (63.26 m2 g-1) suggested that several imprinted cavities were blocked by the encoded CDs.

Considering the occupation of binding sites, the adsorption capacities of CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs for DA were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4C, the binding quality of CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs all decreased with increasing amounts CDs, but those of CD-MIPs were still much higher than those of CD-NIPs. When the amount of CDs were 200 μL, the imprinted factor (denoted by IF) achieved a maximum value, since some non-specific sites were attached to few CDs and decreased the non-specific adsorption for DA. According to this result, 200 μL CDs were used as the optimal quantity in further experiments.

Stability is a key feature for long-time storage. Physical stability maintaining the fluorescent structure and chemical stability preventing decomposition are the main concerns for fluorescent microspheres [47]. The photostability of CD-MIPs was estimated by repeated measurement of fluorescence intensity every hour under continuous irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp for 10 h. As shown in Fig. S11A, the fluorescence intensity hardly decreased and remained as high as 94.9% of the initial intensity, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) < 3.14%. This could contribute to the high photoluminescence stability of carbon dots [48].

According to the literature [18], QDs could leach out from the beads if the beads were dispersed in organic solvents. Therefore, the chemical stability (expressed as leaking percentage) of CD-MIPs was tested using the followed procedure: 1.0 mg of CD-MIPs was suspended into 1 mL of ethanol under ultrasonication for 5 min. After centrifuging for 5 min at 10,000 rpm, the microspheres and supernatant were collected separately, and the microspheres were repeatedly soaked in 1 mL of ethanol to record the fluorescence intensity. From Fig. S11B, only a few CDs, less than 2%, leached out of the microspheres, indicating that non-covalent interaction between microspheres and CDs afforded a stable structure and prevented leaking of carbon dots.
The detection conditions with the effect of pH, adsorption time, and incubation time were evaluated in detailed, as shown in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CD-MIPs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimum conditions, the effect of different concentrations of DA on the fluorescence responses of CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs were studied (see Fig. 5 ). As previously reported [33], the relationship could be quantified using the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F =
K
S V
C
D A
+ 1
Where F 0 and F are fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of DA, respectively. K SV is the quenching constant, which is equal to the imprinted factor. C DA represents the concentration of DA. From the calibration curve displayed in Fig. 5B and 5D, the Stern-Volmer equations are F 0/F = 0.0046C DA + 1 and F 0/F = 0.0006C DA + 1 for CD-MIPs and CD-NIPs, respectively. The imprinted factor of CD-MIPs is about 7.7 times, and a good linear relationship of CD-MIPs sensor was obtained for concentrations of DA ranging from 5 to 300 μg L-1. The limit of detection (LOD) was 2 μg L-1, which was measured by decreasing the DA concentration until the fluorescence intensity was no difference with pure CD-MIPs in PBS buffer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-emission ratiometric sensor for quantification detection of DA
ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric sensor with two or more well resolved individual emission peaks could minimize the background interference, improve analytical accuracy and sensitive by the self-calibration [41]. Based on the high stability, sensitivity and selectivity of CDs-MIPs from the above result, ratiometric sensor for the DA detection was further developed, using the blue emission N-GQD-BIPs as reference signal and red emission 605-MIPs as response signal. From the fluorescence spectra in Fig. S14, the mixture of N-GQD-BIPs and 605-MIPs exhibited individually dual-emission peaks at 445 and 605 nm under 330 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratio optimization of N-GQD-BIPs and 605-MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: To perform the spectral measurement, the fluorescent response of different dual-emission fluorescence probe for DA was evaluated, by changing the mass concentration ratio of N-GQD-BIPs and 605-MIPs from 1:1 to 1:9, and corresponding to the fluorescence intensity ratio from 3:1 to 1:3. The highest quenching efficiency was achieved when the mass concentration ratio of N-GQD-BIPs and 605-MIPs was 1:6 (see Fig. S15). Hence, the dual-emission fluorescent probe with a mixture of 0.03 mg mL-1 N-GQD-BIPs and 0.18 mg mL-1 605-MIPs for the spectral measurement was established throughout the work.

The fluorescent response of dual-emission MIPs and NIPs probe under the addition of different amount DA were examined as the same detection conditions of CD-MIPs sensor, as shown in Fig. 6 . With the increasing of DA concentration, the fluorescent intensity of 605 nm emission peak gradually decreased, while the blue intensity at 445 nm almost remained constant. The value of △(I445/I605) [△(I445/I605)= (I445/I605)-(I445/I605)0, where (I445/I605) and (I445/I605)0 were fluorescence ratio in the presence and absence of DA, respectively] for MIPs sensor was linear increased with increasing DA concentrations in the range from 1 to 100 μg L-1 with a coefficient of 0.9961. The LOD was 0.5 μg L-1 by decreasing the DA concentration until the fluorescence intensity at 605 nm was no difference with that of pure 605 nm in PBS buffer.

In general, some biologically relevant coexisting species could also lead to fluorescence quenching of polymers. The selectivity experiment of two-dual fluorescence sensors were all studied as shown in Fig. 7 . First, some common ions (Na+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cl-, NO3 - and SO4 2-) were introduced to evaluate the quenching effect, as shown in Fig. 7A and C. For the CD-MIPs sensor, only Mg2+, K+ and SO4 2− had a slight fluorescence quenching effect, while DA (F 0/F = 2.36) produced the highest fluorescence quenching effect of all. Similar results were obtained for an evaluation of the effect of various sugars and common amino acids on fluorescence response for CD-MIPs sensor. Based on the employment of reference signal, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited stronger anti-interference ability. The fluorescence response of DA (△(I445/I605) = 1.55) was much higher than that of other interfering substances (-1.91-0.07).

Except for these coexisting species, control experiments for EP, AA, UA, CA, urea and PEA were also conducted at the same time. EP, CA and PEA have similar structures with DA, while AA and UA are commonly co-existed in the brain, and urea is the abundant component of urine. From Fig. 7B and D, fluorescence response of the two-mode sensors exhibited the highest change after the addition of DA, while a negligible change was performed by the addition other interfering compounds. These results are all due to the template molecule of DA exactly fitted imprinted site, while these interfering compounds are not complementary with the imprinted cavities, just depend on non-specific adsorption. Considering the high selectivity and specificity, two-mode sensors all could be selected as an ideal candidate sensor for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the practical applicability, the CD-MIPs and dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor were used to detect the level of DA in human urine, pork kidney and rabbit serum. The accuracy of developed method was evaluated, using the spiked samples for the recovery experiment. Good recoveries in the range of 71.74%–108.63% with RSDs below 9.43% and 71.78%–99.75% with RSDs below 7.77% were obtained for CD-MIPs sensor and ratiometric sensor, respectively (see Table S1). Compared with other reported methods (listed in Table 1 ), our developed methods not only provided dual mode fluorescence detection methods, but also a relatively lower limit and wider linear range. These results suggested that the accuracy of our developed sensors were well, and it was trustworthy for the detection and monitoring the level of DA concentration in real samples.

In summary, we present a novel fluorescence encoded strategy to prepare QDs encoded molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing systems with excellent spectral properties, specific adsorption for template (Dopamine, DA used as model template), and excellent water-compatibility. Compared with the conventional preparation of fluorescent sensors, three overwhelming advantages offered by our study should be emphasized. First, the water-compatible MIPs with high specific surface enabled to capture the different polar QDs as embedding matrix, excellent solving the limitation of solvent systems in preparation of MIPs. Meanwhile, the specific adsorption of template for encoded fluorescent MIPs was still existed in complex biological samples, expanding the application of fluorescent sensors in biological systems. Second, the multi-object detection and accurate quantitative detection could be easily achieved by directly mixed the fluorescent MIPs with different signal. Third, from the perspective of applicable scope, our developed method has a wider scope of application than other methods. The currently reported methods for DA detection are single analytical system. In contrast, we simultaneously proposed 15 fluorescent sensing systems. In a word, we proved the feasibility of the fabrication of fluorescent sensing systems with different color and the application on quantitative detection, suggesting its promise for high-throughput multiple analytes at one time.

This work was supported by “The National Key R&D Program of China” (No. 2016YFD 0401202); The Innovation of research methods and demonstration of safety and quality control of dairy chain (No. 2016IM030400); The Special Project of Tianjin Innovation Platform (No.17PTGCCX00230); Tianjin science and technology planning project (18PTSYJC00130).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank LetPub (www.letpub.com) for providing linguistic assistance during the preparation of this manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127265.

Synthetic process of CDs, N-GQDs, and PHEMA; sample collection and pretreatment; binding mechanism of template and functional monomer; process of RAFT; characterization of polymers; quenching and detection mechanism; optimization of CD-MIPs sensor for the DA detection conditions; Figures showing the chemical structure of analyte in Fig. S1; the UV adsorption of dopamine under different ratio of template and functional monomer in Fig. S2; process of RAFT in Fig. S3 and S4; characterization of polymers in Fig. S5; fluorescent intensity of different batches encoded microspheres in Fig. S6; SEM images of QDs encoded MIPs in Fig. S7; fluorescence response of different encoded MIPs under DA concentrations in Fig. S8; the UV adsorption spectra of DA and emission spectrum of encoded MIPs in Fig. S9; the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of CD-MIPs in Fig. S10; the stability of CD-MIPs in Fig. S11; the conditions optimum of CD-MIPs sensor in Fig. S12; the UV–vis spectra of DA solution after incubation different times in Fig. S13; the fluorescence spectra of N-GQD-BIPs, 605-MIPs and mixture in PBS buffer in Fig. S14; the ratio optimum of dual-emission ratiometric sensor in Fig. S15; Table S1 listing the recoveries of detection DA in different samples.

Talanta.

Sens. Actuators. B.

Talanta.

Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe
Sens and Actuators B-Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Weng, D. Liang, H.Z. Qiu, Z.G. Liu, Z. Lin, Z.F. Zheng, A.L. Liu, W. Chen, X.H. Lin, A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical. 221(2015) 7-14.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaolei Zhao is currently a PhD student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of science and Technology. Her researchers mainly focus on the development and application of molecularly imprinted polymers.
Yan Cui is currently a master student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches focus on the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers.
Yu He is currently a PhD student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researchers mainly focus on the development and application of fluorescence sensors.

Shuo Wang received his Ph.D. in 1999 from The University of Sydney, now he is serving as a professor on Nankai University. He has long been engaged in the research fields in Food Safety and Food Science.

Junping Wang received his Ph.D. in 2003 from China Agricultural University, now he is serving as a professor in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His researchers mainly focus on Food Safety and Food Science.
